Facts.text: Mass execution of population (between 2,500 and 5,000) of Cesena, Italy, by Breton troops of Giovanni Acuto under the command of Robert, Cardinal of Geneva, acting as the legate of Pope Gregory XI
Battle of Diu: Portuguese Armada defeats a Mamluk Egyptian fleet in a naval battle at the port of Diu, India,  establishing Portuguese trading control in the Indian Ocean
German monarchy forms Protestant Union of Torgau
French Chief Minister Cardinal Mazarin returns to Paris from exile
General George Monck's army reaches London
Charles de Bourbon, King of Naples, invites Jews to return to Sicily
Philadelphia establishes a "pesthouse" to quarantine immigrants
Dutch States-General forbids export of windmills
Dutch West Indies island of St Eustatia taken by British
Spain recognizes US independence

A British military force, under Brigadier-General Sir Samuel Auchmuty captures the city of Montevideo in the Battle of Montevideo, then part of the Spanish Empire now capital of Uruguay (Napoleonic Wars)
Territory of Illinois organizes (including present-day Wisconsin)
World's first commercial cheese factory established, in Switzerland
Dutch North Sea coast floods
The sovereignty of Greece confirmed in a London Protocol
Wake Forest University is established in North Carolina
Whig Party holds its 1st national convention (Albany NY)
Wisconsin Supreme Coutt declares US Fugitive Slave Law unconstitutional
Early ice hockey game played between teams from Swavesey and Over on Mare Fen, England
Thomas Clemson takes office as 1st US superintendent of agriculture
Hampton Roads Peace Conference, Lincoln & Stephens reach an impasse
Prince Mutsuhito, 14, becomes Emperor Meiji of Japan (1867-1912)
Booth Theater at 23rd & 6th opens in NYC (Romeo & Juliet)
Iowa ratifies the 15th Amendment to the United States Constitution, allowing suffrage for all races and colors
Albert Spalding invests $800 to start a sporting goods company, manufacturing the first official baseball, tennis ball, basketball, golf ball, and football
To avoid disputed national elections, the US Congress creates Electoral Count Act
Russia closes down the Volozhin Yeshiva, a Lithuanian talmudical college
1st US steel sailing vessel, Dirigo, launched at Bath, Maine
Wilhelm Mauseth skates world record 500 m (46.8 secs)
Gubernatorial candidate William Goebel is assassinated in Frankfort, Kentucky
Rival forces fight for control of the Union Park ball grounds in Baltimore
Dutch troops under Gen Van Heutsz conquer Batu Ilië on Sumatra
Frederick Lugard occupies Kano, West Africa
Foundation of Panathinaikos in Athens, Greece.
Supreme Court rules a union boycott violates Sherman Antitrust Act
16th Amendment to the US Constitution instituting a federal income tax is ratified
Turkish & German army reach Suez Canal
Canada's original Parliament buildings in Ottawa burn down
Tristan Tzara publishes the Dada manifesto in Zurich, Switzerland
Twin Peaks Tunnel for streetcars begins service in San Francisco, at 11,920 feet one of world's longest
Socialist conference convenes (Berne, Switzerland)
The Bolshevik army is defeated in a series of clashes with the White Russians, who are fighting to reclaim the government after the 1917 revolution
Soviet Commisision of Commasars (Sovnarkom) approves plans for expanding the Red Air Fleet mail service to include passenger service, seen as the country's start of civil aviation
Soviet Council of People's Commissars (Sovnarkom) approves plans for expanding the Red Air Fleet mail service to include passenger service, seen as the country's start of civil aviation
The Alpha Zeta chapter of Phi Mu Alpha Sinfonia founded at Pennsylvania State University.
Alexei Ryko elected as President of People's commission (succeeds Lenin)
Switzerland claims the inaugural Olympic bobsleigh gold medal at the Chamonix Games, ahead of Great Britain and Belgium
Uprising against regime of general Carmona in Portugal
Revolutionary Socialist Party forms in Amsterdam
The Indochinese Communist Party of Vietnam is established
1st interstate legislative conference in US opens, Washington, D.C.
US Supreme Court upheld Federal Wage & Hour law, sets minimum wages & maximum hours
1st Japanese air raid on Java
Baseball owners agree to permit each club up to 14 night games in 1942
"The Four Chaplains" drown after giving up their life jackets to others as the US Army transport ship Dorchester sinks in the North Atlantic
World War II: United States troops capture the Marshall Islands.
Almost 1,000 Flying Fortresses drop 3,000 tons of bombs on Berlin
Walt Disney's "3 Caballeros" released
-81°F (-63°C), Snag Yukon (North American record)
1st black reporter in US Congressional press gallery (Percival Prattis)
Largest purse to date in horse racing, $144,323, won by Great Circle
J Fred Muggs, a chimpanzee, becomes a regular on NBC's Today Show
Jeen van den Berg wins Dutch Eleven Cities Skating race (7:32)
Autherine Lucy admitted to University of Alabama, and suspended for her own safety a few days later after a riot by protesters on campus
Toni Sailer of Austria wins the downhill at the Cortina d'Ampezzo Winter Olympics; becomes first athlete to sweep all 3 alpine skiing events in a single Olympics
Royal Teens' "Short Shorts" enters Top 40 chart & peaks at #3
American Airlines Flight 320 crashes in NY's East River, killing 65 of the 73 people on board the Lockheed L-188 Electra
6th largest snowfall in NYC history (17.4 inches or 44.2cm)
John Uelses pole vaults record 489 cm
"Meet the Beatles" album goes gold
Black & Puerto Rican students boycott NYC public schools

French sisters Marielle Goitschel (gold) and Christine Goitschel (silver) repeat (in reverse order) their top-2 finish in the slalom 2 days earlier, in the giant slalom at the Innsbruck Winter Olympics
105 USAF cadets resigned for cheating in exams
Braves offer Milwaukee $500,000 to terminate their lease a year earlier, the proposal is turned down
Geraldine McCullough wins Widener Gold Medal for Sculpture
Orbiting Solar Observatory 2 launches into Earth orbit (552/636 km)
1st operational weather satellite, ESSA-1 launched US
First soft landing on the Moon, by the Soviet spacecraft Luna 9
Ronald Ryan, the last person to be executed in Australia, is hanged in Pentridge Prison, Melbourne
Ibuprofen launched in the UK under brand name Brufen as prescription medicine - now world's most popular anti-inflammatory painkiller
A series of house searches by the British Army in Catholic areas of Belfast, resulting in serious rioting and gun battles
KTSC TV channel 8 in Pueblo-Colorado Spgs, CO (PBS) 1st broadcast
NYPD officer Frank Serpico is shot during a drug bust while his fellow officers stood outside and failed to call for assistance
OPEC mandates "total embargo" against any company that rejects 55 percent tax rate
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
XI Winter Olympic Games opens in Sapporo, Japan (1st in Asia)
Dr. Hook & the Medicine Show's single "The Cover of the 'Rolling Stone'", written by Shel Silverstein, enters the top 40 and peaks at #6
Martin Dihigo  and John "Pop" Lloyd are elected to Baseball Hall of Fame
Australia beat India 3-2 on 6th day of final test, after India are all out for 445 chasing 493 to win
"Y.M.C.A." by Village People peaks at #2 on pop singles chart
Mohammed Ali tours Africa as President Carter's envoy
Gro Harlem Brundtland is elected as the first female Prime Minister of Norway
Columbia Shuttle moves to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida) for mating for STS-3 mission
Greatest helicopter lift, 56,888 kg, Podmoscovnoe, USSR
John Sharples of England finishes 371 hours of disco dancing
10th NASA Space Shuttle Mission (41B): Challenger 4 launches
OPEC fails to agree upon a petroleum production agreement after a 2 day meeting in Vienna
MLB Montreal Expos trade pitcher Jeff Reardon to Minnesota Twins for pitcher Neal Heaton
Nurses across the UK strike over pay and funding for the NHS
Baseball's National League announces Yanks' broadcaster Bill White will its 1st African American President
Labor strike at Royal Canadian Mint ends
Maximum NY State unemployment benefits raised to $300 per week
General Hospital actor Tristan Rogers (46) convicted of drunk driving in Beverly Hills, California
MLB Cincinnati Reds owner Marge Schott suspended for 1 year due to racist comments
STS-60 (Discovery) launches into orbit
STS 63 (Discovery 19), launches into orbit
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Detroit< Michigan on WKRK 97.1 FM
Cavalese cable car disaster: 20 people killed after NATO aircraft severs a cable car line
Former teacher Mary Kay LeTourneau (36) violates probation with the 14 year-old father of her baby
Karla Faye Tucker is executed in Texas, first woman executed in the United States since 1984
NY Yankees replace general manager Bob Watson with Brian Cashman
In Jammu and Kashmir the political party Democratic Janata Dal (Jammu and Kashmir) is revived.
A Baghdad market bombing kills at least 135 people and injures a further 339.
All available blocks of IPv4 internet addresses are officially distributed to regional authorities.
33 people are killed by a suicide bombing by an explosive-packed truck in Kirkuk, Iraq
Super Bowl XLVII, Mercedes-Benz Stadium, New Orleans, LA: Baltimore Ravens beat San Francisco 49ers, 34-31; MVP: Joe Flacco, Baltimore, QB
Teenage student shoots and kills a geography teacher and a police officer, and wounds 8 at School 263 on the outskirts of Moscow, Russia
Lord Lucan's death certificate is granted, 42 years after he disappeared following the murder of nanny Sandra Rivett
Moscow, Russia has its heaviest snowfall in a day on record (43 cm / 17 inches), killing one and bringing down 2,000 trees
British-born rapper 21 Savage arrested by US immigration for overstaying his visa
Dam in Townsville, Australia, deliberately opened flooding 20,000 homes after city receives record 3.3ft of rain in a week
Super Bowl LIII, Mercedes-Benz Stadium, Atlanta, GA: New England Patriots beat Los Angeles Rams, 13-3; MVP: Julian Edelman, NE Patriots, WR; Patriots' 6th SB victory
Wreckage from light aircraft carrying EPL team Cardiff City's record signing Emiliano Sala is discovered on seabed of the English Channel; cause of death, head and trunk injuries
Cruise ship Diamond Princess with 3700 passengers quarantined in Yokohama port, Japan after cases of COVID-19 found on board
Malawi's constitutional court annuls 2019 election which saw President Peter Mutharika re-elected due to voting irregularities
US and Russia extend their New START nuclear arms control treaty for five years, the last remaining nuclear arms deal between the two countries
Austria brings into force its vaccine mandate for all, first country in Europe to do so
ISIS leader Abu Ibrahim al-Hashimi al-Qurayshi blows himself and his family up during a raid by US Special Forces in Syria
Coldest wind chill likely ever recorded in the US of minus 108 degrees, taken at Mount Washington Observatory, New Hampshire
Train carrying toxic chemicals including vinyl chloride, derails in East Palestine, Ohio, starting a fire and leading to an intentional burn off of chemicals to stop a possible explosion
Edmond Dédé's opera "Morgiane" world premiere performance, 138 years after its composition, by the OperaCréole and Opera Lafayette at the Lincoln Theatre in Washington, D.C.
Sultan Mehmed II, the Conqueror inherits the throne of the Ottoman Empire
Bartolomeu Diasand his crew are the first Europeans to land on South African soil at Mossel Bay (east of modern Cape Town)
Henry of Navarre (futureHenry IV) escapes from Paris
Most elaborate masque of the Carolinian age - "The Triumph of Peace" held at Whitehall Palace, London, in front of KingCharles I, written by James Shirley with sets by Inigo Jones
Hector Berlioz's overture "Carnaval Romain" premieres  at the Salle Herz in Paris
Samuel Clemens first uses the pen nameMark Twainin a Virginia City newspaper, the "Territorial Enterprise"
US Civil War: Union ArmyGeneral Shermanbegins his Meriden Campaign to disrupt Confederate railroads through Mississippi in Vicksburg
Circus ownerP. T. Barnumbuys his world-famous elephant Jumbo
Josek Suk's "Asreal Symphony", dedicated to his late wife and  his father-in-law Antonin Dvořák, premieres at the Prague National Theatre, conducted by Karel Kovařovic
US ocean liner Housatonic is sunk by a German submarine, on the same day that US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonbreaks off diplomatic relations with Germany

PaleoanthropologistDavidson Blackreports his findings on the ancient human fossils found at Zhoukoudian, China, in the journal Nature and declares them to be a new species he names 'Sinanthropus pekinensis' (now known as 'Homo erectus')
William Howard Taftresigns as US chief justice for health reasons
Arkansas legislature passes a motion to pray for the soul of journalistH. L. Menckenafter he calls the state the "apex of moronia"
"Shanghai Express" directed by Josef von Sternberg and starringMarlene Dietrich, Clive Brook and Anna Mae Wong premieres in Los Angeles
Dutch CommunistMarinus van der Lubbedeparts for Berlin (later arrested, convicted and executed for supposedly setting fire to the German Reichstag)
German MinisterHermann Goeringbans social-democratic newspaper "Vorwarts"
Nuclear physicistKlaus Fuchsarrested on spying charges
"TheVictor BorgeShow" debuts on NBC-TV
Tennessee Williams' play "The Rose Tattoo" premieres in NYC
Plane crash known as "The Day the Music Died" kills musiciansBuddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, J. P. Richardson (aka The Big Bopper), and the pilot near Clear Lake, Iowa
"La Dolce Vita" directed byFederico Felliniand starring Marcello Mastroianni andAnita Ekberghas its film premiere in Italy
British Prime MinisterHarold Macmillanmakes his famous "wind of change" speech in Africa, against the apartheid regime, angering South African politicians
US PresidentJohn F. Kennedybans all trade with Cuba except for food & drugs
Jimi Hendrixadds vocals and overdubs for "Purple Haze" at Olympic Studios in London; basic track had been recorded by the band in January
Northern Ireland Prime MinisterTerence O'Neillannounces the dissolution of the Stormont parliament and the holding of new elections
Billy Herman, Earl Averill, &Bucky Harriselected to Baseball Hall of Fame
Egyptian President Anwar al-Sadat and US PresidentJimmy Carterdiscuss the Middle East peace process in Washington, D.C.
Minnesota Twins tradeRod Carewto California for 4 players
Larry HolmesTKOs Lorenzo Holmes in 6 for heavyweight boxing title
Pixar Animation Studios (Toy Story; The Incredibles) headed by Edwin Catmull and Alvy Ray Smith, is spun off from Lucasfilm, Ltd as an independent film production company, with backing ofSteve Jobs
The Pope andMother Teresameet in Calcutta
US PresidentRonald Reaganannounces formation of commission of enquiry on Challenger Accident
US Senate confirmsAnthony Kennedyto seat on US Supreme Court by a 97-0 vote
Military coup overthrows Paraguayan DictatorAlfredo Stroessnerwho flees to Brazil
American jockeyBill Shoemaker(58), retires after his 40,350 horse race, he continues his career as a horse trainer
Darryl Strawberryvoluntarily enters Smither Center for Alcohol rehabilitation
Defense opens callingNoriega"our ally in war on drugs"
PresidentBill Clintonlifts US trade embargo against Vietnam
Public memorial held forCarl Saganat Cornell University, Ithaca, NY
Florida PantherDino Ciccarelliis 9th NHLer to score 600 career goals
Stamps commemoratingDiana, Princess of Wales, go on sale in Britain
Super Bowl XXXVI, Louisiana Superdome, New Orleans, LA: New England Patriots beat St. Louis Rams, 20-17; MVP:Tom Brady, New England, QB
Actress Lana Clarkson is shot and killed byPhil Spectorat Spector's house in Alhambra, California
Super Bowl XLII, University of Phoenix Stadium, Glendale, AZ: New York Giants beat New England Patriots, 17-14; MVP:Eli Manning, New York, QB
Eric Holderbecomes 82nd and 1st African American US Attorney General, succeeding Michael Mukasey
Léa Seydouxreceives the Lumières Award for Best Actress for the film "Grand Central"
Rand Pauldrops out of the Republican Presidential nomination race
US PresidentBarack Obamavisits his first US mosque - the Islamic Society of Baltimore mosque in Maryland
Pope Francisarrives in Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates, on the first ever papal visit to the Arabian peninsula
First Democratic caucus in Iowa won narrowly by Pete Buttigieg (most delegates) andBernie Sanders(most votes) after lengthy delays and irregularities in reporting the results
Canada beats United States, 6-1 to retain the Olympic ice hockey gold medal (won at 1920 Summer Games) at the Chamonix Games; Canadian LW Harry Watson top scores with 46 points
New Zealand's worst natural disaster, the Hawke's Bay earthquake, kills 256 and injures thousands, devastating Napier and the Hawke's Bay region
WWII: British Prime MinisterWinston Churchillarrives in Yalta, Crimea, Soviet Union for the "Big Three" Yalta Conference
Federal trial of 4 police officers charged with civil rights violations in videotaped beating ofRodney Kingbegins in Los Angeles, California
Joanna of Bourbon, wife of Charles V of France, born in Château de Vincennes, France (d. 1378)
Henry Percy, 2nd Earl of Northumberland, English nobleman and military leader, born at Alnwick Castle, Northumberland, England (d. 1455)
John Hamilton, Scottish prelate (Archbishop of St Andrews, 1547-71), and politician, born in Stirling Scotland (d. 1571)
Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina, Italian composer, born in Palestrina, Papal States (d. 1594) [birthday unconfirmed]
Louis Bouteiller, French composer, born in Moncé-en-Belin, France (d. 1725)
François Fagel, Dutch field marshal (War of the Spanish Succession), born in Nijmegen, baptized date (d. 1718)
Jan Santini Aichel, Czech Baroque Gothic architect, born in Prague, Bohemia (d. 1723)
Nicholas Cooke, American mariner, slave trader, and politician (1st Governor of Rhode Island, 1776-78), born in Providence, British Colony of Rhode Island (d. 1782)
Friedrich Wilhelm, baron von Seydlitz, Prussian general, born in Kalkar, Germany l (d. 1773)
Johann Georg Albrechtsberger, Austrian organist and composer (trained Beethoven), born in Klosterneuburg Austria-Hungary (d. 1809)
Guillaume Lasceux, French organist and composer, born in Poissy, France (d. 1831)
Samuel Osgood, American patriot, 4th Postmaster of the US, born in Andover, Massachusetts (d. 1813)
Florido Toméoni, Italian composer, and playwright, born in Camaiore, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1820)
Caroline von Wolzogen [von Lengefeld], German author, born in Rudolstadt (d. 1847)
John Fane, British politician, diplomat (11th Earl of Westmoreland), and composer (Royal Academy of Music, 1830), born in Piccadilly, London, England (d. 1859)
Antonio José de Sucre, South American independence leader as chief lieutenant to Simón Bolívar, born in Cumaná, New Granada (d. 1830)
Otto Theodor von Manteuffel, Prussian conservative statesman and Minister-President of the Kingdom of Prussia (1850-88), born in Lübben (Spreewald), Electorate of Saxony (d. 1882)
Ansel Briggs, American politician, 1st Governor of Iowa (1846-50), born in Shoreham, Vermont (d. 1881)
Horace Greeley, American founder and editor of the New-York Tribune ("Go west, young man"), born in Amherst, New Hampshire (d. 1872)
Achille Delesse, French geologist and mineralogist, born in Metz, France (d. 1881)
Emile Prudent, French pianist and composer, born in Angoulême, France (d. 1863)
Samuel Ryan Curtis, American politician and Major General (Union Army), born in Champlain, New York (d. 1866)
Elisha Kent Kane, US Arctic naval surgeon and Artic explorer (led expedition to Greenland discover fate of John Franklin), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1857)
Spencer Fullerton Baird, American naturalist and ornithologist (Wood's Hole Station), born in Reading, Pennsylvania (d. 1888)
George Thomas "Tige" Anderson, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Covington, Georgia (d. 1901)
Nathan George "Shanks" Evans, American Brigadier-General (Confederate States Army), born in Marion County, South Carolina (d. 1868)
Ranald MacDonald, North American-born educator and interpreter, 1st English speaker to teach English in Japan, born in Fort Astoria, Columbia District, British North America (d. 1894)
Walter Bagehot, English journalist and economist (founded National Review), born in Langport, England (d. 1877)
Cyrus Ballou Comstock, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Wrentham, Massachusetts (d. 1910)
Thomas Theodoor Hendrikus Jorissen, Dutch historian, born in Utrecht, the Netherlands (d. 1889)
Georges Jacobi, German violinist, composer (The Black Crook), and conductor (Alhambra Theatre, London, 1872-98), born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1906)
Sidney Lanier, American poet and composer, born in Macon, Georgia (d. 1881)
Ernst von Wildenbruch, German playwright (Sedan), born in Beirut in Lebanon (d. 1909)
George Harris, (4th Baron Harris), English cricket batsman and captain (4 Tests, 1 x 50; Kent CCC), born in St Ann's, Trinidad (d. 1932)
Joannes Benedictus van Heutsz, Dutch general (ended Aceh War) and Governor General of Dutch East Indies 1904-09), born in Coevorden, Netherlands (d. 1924)
Wilhelm Ludvig Johannsen, Danish Botanist and Geneticist (provided evidence for Hugo de Vries' mutation theory), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1927)
Hugo Junkers, German engineer and aircraft designer, born in Rheydt, Rhine Province, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1935)
Martinus Ballings, Belgian Jesuit author (Wilskracht/Willpower), born in Tongerlo, Belgium (d. 1958)[1]
Dámaso Ledesma, Spanish organist, composer, musicologist, and priest, born in Ciudad Rodrigo, Spain (d. 1928)
Ada Negri, Italian poet and author (Il Libro di Mara), born in Lodi, Italy (d. 1945)
Franklin Dyall, British actor (Easy Virtue, Atlantic), born in Liverpool, England (d. 1950)
Ernest-Bernard Allo, French Dominican theologist, born in Quintin, France (d. 1945)
Gertrude Stein, American-French writer (Autobiography of Alice B. Toklas), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1946)
William Tedmarsh, British silent movie actor (Two Beds and No Sleep), born in London, England (d. 1937)
Joseph Gordon Coates, Prime Minister of New Zealand (1925-28), born in Ruatuna, New Zealand (d. 1943)
Charles Follis "The Black Cyclone", American football halfback (1st black pro player Shelby Blues 1902-06), born in Cloverdale, Virginia (d. 1910)
Trijntje Jansma-Boskma; Dutch centenarian, oldest Dutch person at 109 years at her death, born in Veenwoudsterwal, Netherlands (d. 1991)
Camille Bombois, French circus wrestler and naïve painter, born in Venarey-les-Laumes, France (d. 1970)
Clarence E. Mulford, American writer (Hopalong Cassidy), born in Streator, Illinois (d. 1956)
Erwin Kroll, German composer, born in Eylau, German Empire (now Iława, Poland) (d. 1976)
Carlo Jachino, Italian composer, born in San Remo, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1971)
Georg Trakl, Austrian poet (Autumn of the Lonely), born in Salzburg, Austria (d. 1914)
Juan Negrín, Spanish Republican Prime Minister during the Civil War (1937-39), born in Las Palmas, Canary Islands (d. 1956)
Silvio D'Amico, Italian theater critic (Tramonto del grande attore), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1955)
Joris Baers, Belgian priest and librarian (founded magazine Boekengids), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1975)
Carl Theodor Dreyer, Danish director (Passion of Jeanne d'arc), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1968)
Risto Ryti, Finnish politician (Prime Minister of Finland 1939-41, President of Finland 1940-44), born in Huittinen, Finland (d. 1956)
Charles Correll, American radio comedian (Amos 'n' Andy), born in Preoria, Illinois (d. 1972)
Larry MacPhail, American Baseball Hall of Fame executive (Cincinnati Reds, Brooklyn Dodgers, New York Yankees), born in Cass City, Michigan (d. 1975)
Jan Donner, Dutch minister of Justice (1926-33) and Minister of State, born in Assen, Netherlands (d. 1981)
Gaston Julia, Algerian-French mathematician known for his work on rational functions (Julia Sets), born in Sidi bel Abbès, Algeria (d. 1978)
Norman Rockwell, American artist and illustrator (Saturday Evening Post covers), born in New York City (d. 1978)
Nick Kenny, American columnist and songwriter (Nick Kenny Show), born in New York City (d. 1975)
Victor Raul Haya de la Torre, Peruvian politician (founded APRA-Peru, drafted constitution), born in Trujillo, Peru (d. 1979) [or Feb 22]
Johannes Urzidil, German-Bohemian poet and historian, born in Prague (d. 1970)
Alvar Aalto, Finnish architect (Finlandia House) and designer, born in Kuortane, Finland (d. 1976)
Lillian "Lil" Hardin Armstrong, American jazz pianist, composer, and bandleader, born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1971)
Pavel Urysohn, Ukrainian mathematician, born in Odessa, Russian Empire (d. 1924)
Café Filho, Brazilian politician, President of Brazil (1954-55), born in Natal, Brazil (d. 1970)
Doris Speed, British actress dubbed 'The Queen Mother of Soap' (Coronation Street), born in Manchester, England (d. 1994)
Forrest DeBernardi, American Basketball HOF center (5 × AAU C'ship; 5 × AAU All-American Westminster College), born in Nevada, Missouri (d. 1970)
Lao She, Chinese writer and playwright (Rickshaw Boy, Teahouse), born in Beijing, China (d. 1966)
Mabel Mercer, British jazz and cabaret singer whose style and phrasing influenced Frank Sinatra ("Fly Me To The Moon"), born in Burton upon Trent, England (d. 1984)
(Ivy) Priaulx Rainier, South African-British composer (Ploërmel), born in Howick, British Colony of Natali (d. 1986)
Walter Linck, Swiss sculptor (1948 Olympics), born in Bern, Switzerland (d. 1975)
Jimmy Starr, American screenwriter, actor (The Corpse Came C.O.D.), and gossip columnist, born in Clarksville, Texas, (d. 1990)
Luigi Dallapiccola, Italian 12-tone composer (Il prigioniero; Ulisse), born in Pisino d'Istria, Austrian Empire (now Pazin, Croatia) (d. 1975)
Roger Makins, British diplomat (UK Ambassador to the US 1953-56), born in London (d. 1996)
Arne Beurling, Swedish mathematician known for The "Beurling factorization" and for decrypting a German cipher machine during WWII, born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 1986)
George Adamson [Baba ya Simba - "Father of Lions"], British conservationist (Born Free), born in Etawah, India (d. 1989)
Ludvig Nielsen, Norwegian organist, composer, choir director, and conductor (Nidaros Cathedral, 1935-76), born in Borge, Norway (d. 2001)
James A. Michener, American author (Tales of the South Pacific, Hawaii), born in Doylestown, Pennsylvania (d. 1997)
Oddbjørn Hagen, Norwegian nordic skier (Olympic gold cross country individual 1936; World C'ship gold 1934, 35), born in Ytre Rendal, Norway (d. 1983)
Andre Cayatte, French filmmaker, born in Carcassonne, France (d. 1989)
Simone Weil, French philosopher, Resistance fighter (WWII) and social activist, born in Paris, France (d. 1943)
Blas Galindo, Mexican composer (A la Patria), born in San Gabriel, Mexico (d. 1993)
Nelson Case, American TV host (Trash or Treasure), born in Long Beach, California (d. 1976)
Robert Earl Jones, American actor (The Sting, Witness, Sleepaway Camp), born in Senatobia, Mississippi (d. 2006)
Jehan Alain, French organist and composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1940)
Jesse "Babyface" Thomas, American blues singer-songwriter and guitarist ("Blue Goose Blues"), born in Logansport, Louisiana (d. 1995)
Tom Creavy, American golfer (PGA C'ship 1931), born in Tuckahoe, New York (d. 1979)
Jacques Soustelle, French anthropologist and Minister of Information (Free French forces), born in Montpellier, France (d. 1990)
Richard Seaman, British auto racer (German Grand Prix, 1938), born in Chichester, England (d. 1939)
Edward Sinclair, English actor (Dad's Army), born in Lancashire, England (d. 1977)
Felix Kelly, New Zealand-born British artist, born in Auckland (d. 1994)
Kees Andrea, Dutch painter, graphic artist and carpet designer, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 2006)
Bill Miller, American jazz pianist and arranger (Frank Sinatra, Frank Sinatra, Jr.), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2006)
Richard Bales, American composer (National Gallery of Art orchestra), born in Alexandria, Virginia (d. 1998)
Helen Stephens, American athlete (Olympic gold 100m & 4x100m relay 1936), born in Fulton, Missouri (d. 1994)
Joey Bishop [Gottlieb], American TV talk show host (Joey Bishop Show) and member of the "Rat Pack" group of entertainers, born in The Bronx, New York (d. 2007)
(Eugene) "Snooky" Young, American jazz trumpeter who mastered the plunger mute, born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 2011)
Chico Alvarez, Canadian jazz trumpeter (Stan Kenton Orchestra), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1992)
George A. Miller, cognitive psychologist (the magical number seven), born in Charleston, West Virginia (d. 2012)
Henry Heimlich, Surgeon and inventor of the Heimlich maneuver, born in Wilmington, Delaware (d. 2016)
Margaret "Bibi" Osterwald, American actress (As Good as It Gets, Bridget Loves Bernie), born in New Brunswick, New Jersey (d. 2002)
Stan Ockers, Belgian road cyclist (World road champion 1955; Tour de France runner-up 1950, 52), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1956)
Tony Gaze, Australian WWII fighter pilot and auto racer (first Australian to drive in a F1 GP 1951), born in Melbourne, Victoria (d. 2013)
Reg Owen [George Owen Smith], British saxophonist, arranger, conductor, and film score composer, born in Hackney, London (d. 1978)
Alys Robi [Alice Robitaille], French Canadian cabaret and radio singer of French translations of Latin songs, born in Quebec City, Quebec (d. 2011)
John Fiedler, American stage and screen actor (The Bob Newhart Show - "Mr. Peterson"; The Odd Couple - "Vinnie"; 12 Angry Men), born in Platteville, Wisconsin (d. 2005)
Joop Swart, Dutch journalist, photographer, and publisher (founder of World Press Photo), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Leon Schlumpf, Swiss politician, President Swiss Federal Council (1984), born in Felsberg, Switzerland (d. 2012)
Art Arfons, auto racer/designer (3 x world land speed record; "Green Monster"), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 2007)
Glen Tetley, American ballet and modern dancer (Joffrey Ballet), and choreographer (Pierrot Lunaire; Alice), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2007)
